questions.question: Explain the single-level directory structure and state two disadvantages of this approach.
keyPoints: All files stored in single directory
Requires unique filenames for all files
Simple but impractical for large systems

questions.question: What is the difference between absolute path and relative path names? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Absolute path starts from root directory
Relative path starts from current directory
Root denoted by / in UNIX systems

questions.question: Describe the operations that can be performed on a directory in an operating system.
What is a symbolic link? How does it differ from a hard link?
keyPoints: Symbolic link contains path to target file
Hard link cannot cross file systems
Deleting original file breaks symbolic link but not hard link

questions.question: Explain the hierarchical directory structure with the help of a diagram.
What is directory quota? Explain its purpose in multi-user systems.
Describe two methods for implementing directories. Compare their time complexities.
keyPoints: Linear list implementation - simple but O(n) search
Hash table implementation - O(1) average lookup
Linear list: easy to implement, slow for large directories
Hash table: fast lookup, collision handling needed

questions.question: Explain the five types of directory structures: single-level, two-level, hierarchical, acyclic graph, and general graph. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Single-level: one directory for all, simple but name conflicts
Two-level: MFD and UFD per user, user isolation but no subdirectories
Hierarchical: tree structure, excellent organization, supports arbitrary depth
Acyclic graph: allows sharing with links, no cycles, complex traversal
General graph: allows cycles, most flexible but requires cycle detection

questions.question: Explain the concept of directory implementation using hash tables. How are collisions handled? What are the advantages and disadvantages of this approach?
keyPoints: Hash function maps filename to bucket index
Advantages: O(1) average lookup, efficient for large directories
Disadvantages: collision handling overhead, table size management
Fixed-size table may waste space or become congested
Must handle file deletion carefully in hash tables

questions.question: Describe the acyclic graph directory structure. How does it allow file sharing? What are the issues in traversing such a structure and how are they resolved?
keyPoints: Uses links/pointers to reference shared resources
No cycles in the graph structure
Issue: multiple paths to same file (aliasing)
Issue: traversing may visit same directory multiple times
Solution: maintain visited set during traversal
Solution: reference counting for link management

questions.question: Explain the protection mechanisms provided for directories in operating systems. How is access control implemented?
keyPoints: Directory permissions similar to file permissions
Access Control Lists (ACLs) for fine-grained control
In UNIX: rwx permissions for directories
